questions.question: In C, what does `static` at file scope (i.e., outside any function) do?
options: Gives the declaration INTERNAL linkage — the name is visible only inside this translation unit, not exported to the linker
Marks it as a compile-time constant
questions.explanation: File-scope `static` = internal linkage. Without it, the variable has external linkage and any other .c can `extern`-declare it and link. `static` is C's way of saying 'this is implementation detail of this file.' Encapsulation in C.

questions.question: What's the purpose of an include guard like `#ifndef FOO_H / #define FOO_H / ... / #endif`?
options: Prevents multiple inclusions of the same header in one translation unit, which would cause duplicate type/function declarations
Tells the compiler to use a faster path
questions.explanation: Headers often include other headers; without guards, the same struct/function declaration appears multiple times and the compiler errors with 'redefinition.' Include guards make the second-and-later inclusions a no-op. `#pragma once` is a vendor extension that does the same.

questions.question: In a Rust project, what is the difference between a *crate* and a *package*?
options: A crate is a single unit of compilation (one library OR one binary). A package contains one or more crates and is managed by cargo (one Cargo.toml). Most packages contain just one crate.
Crate is for binaries; package is for libraries
questions.explanation: Cargo terminology: a *package* is the project (one Cargo.toml). A *crate* is one compilation target inside it — usually one library and/or one binary. Most everyday Rust packages have one crate; complex projects can have multiple (workspace).

questions.question: Rust's `pub(crate) fn foo()` means:
options: Public to everyone, even external crates
Visible inside this crate, but NOT exported to other crates that depend on this one — internal-API visibility
Only visible in the parent module
questions.explanation: `pub(crate)` = 'internal-API public'. The function/type is reachable from any module in this crate, but downstream crates that pull in your crate as a dependency can't see it. Equivalent of Java's package-private or Kotlin's `internal`. Default `pub` is public to the world.

questions.question: Why do C headers contain DECLARATIONS but C source files contain DEFINITIONS?
options: Definitions can appear in exactly one translation unit (or the linker reports 'multiple definition'). Headers are included by many .c files — so they hold declarations (prototypes) that don't allocate storage or emit code.
questions.explanation: One Definition Rule: each non-inline function or non-extern variable has exactly one definition across the whole program. If a definition lived in a header included by N .c files, you'd have N definitions and a linker error. So headers carry declarations (which can appear many times) and .c files carry definitions (which must appear exactly once).

questions.question: In Go, names exported from a package are determined by:
options: Capitalization: identifiers starting with an uppercase letter are exported (visible outside the package); lowercase ones are package-private
questions.explanation: Go made the unusual choice of using identifier capitalization for visibility: `fmt.Println` is exported (capital P); `fmt.format` would be package-private. Pros: visibility is visible at every call site. Cons: refactoring a name from internal to exported requires touching every reference.
